appearances.context: Fluxus was an international, interdisciplinary community of artists, composers, designers, and poets during the 1960s and 1970s who engaged in experimental art performances which emphasized the artistic process over the finished product.
The origins of Fluxus lie in many of the concepts explored by composer John Cage in his experimental music of the 1930s through the 1960s.
Such perceived insurrections in the coherence of Maciunas' leadership of Fluxus provided an opening for Fluxus to become increasingly influenced by Japanese members of the group.

Fluxus has been defined as a synthesis of three key factors that define the majority of Fluxus work: Fluxus is intermedia.
George Maciunas, largely considered to be the founder of this fluid movement, coined the name Fluxus in 1961 to title a proposed magazine.
Maciunas first publicly coined the term Fluxus in a 'brochure prospectus' that he distributed to the audience at a festival he had organized.

In keeping with the reputation Fluxus earned as a forum of experimentation, some Fluxus artists came to describe Fluxus as a laboratory.

These classes explored the notions of chance and indeterminacy in art, using music scores as a basis for compositions that could be performed in potentially infinite ways.

appearances.context: Another important Fluxus characteristic was the elimination of perceived boundaries between art and life, a very prominent trend in post war art.
